questions.prompt: The Z-transform of a discrete sequence x[n] is defined as:
choices: A. X(z) = Σ x[n] z^n (sum from 0 to ∞)
B. X(z) = Σ x[n] z^(−n) (sum from −∞ to ∞)
questions.explanation: Bilateral Z-transform: X(z) = Σ_{n=−∞}^∞ x[n] z^(−n). Unilateral: n from 0 to ∞.

questions.prompt: The Z-transform of the unit impulse δ[n] is:
questions.explanation: Z{δ[n]} = 1 (only n=0 contributes: δ[0]·z⁰ = 1).

questions.prompt: The Z-transform of the unit step u[n] is:
The Z-transform of aⁿ u[n] is:
questions.explanation: Z{aⁿu[n]} = z/(z−a) for |z| > |a|.

questions.prompt: The Z-transform of a delayed sequence x[n−k] is:
questions.explanation: Time delay: Z{x[n−k]} = z^(−k) X(z) (with zero initial conditions). Delay in discrete time ↔ multiply by z^(−k).

questions.prompt: The final value theorem for Z-transforms gives x[∞] = ?
questions.explanation: FVT for Z: x[∞] = lim_{z→1} (z−1)X(z), valid when all poles of (z−1)X(z) are inside the unit circle.

questions.prompt: For a causal discrete-time LTI system, BIBO stability requires that all poles of H(z) lie:
choices: D. In the left half of the z-plane
questions.explanation: Discrete stability: all poles inside the unit circle |z| < 1. Analogous to left-half s-plane for continuous systems.

questions.prompt: The relationship between the Z-transform and the discrete-time Fourier transform (DTFT) is:
choices: A. DTFT is Z evaluated on the unit circle: X(e^(jω)) = X(z)|_{z=e^(jω)}
B. Z-transform is DTFT with z = e^(−jω)
D. DTFT uses z^n while Z uses e^(−jωn)
questions.explanation: DTFT = Z-transform evaluated on the unit circle z = e^(jω). The Z-transform generalizes the DTFT to complex z.

questions.prompt: A first-order IIR filter has difference equation y[n] = ay[n−1] + bx[n]. Its transfer function H(z) = ?
questions.explanation: Taking Z-transform: Y(z) = az^(−1)Y(z) + bX(z) → H(z) = b/(1−az^(−1)) = bz/(z−a).

questions.prompt: Convolution in discrete time corresponds to _____ in the Z-domain.
questions.explanation: Convolution theorem: Z{x[n]*h[n]} = X(z)·H(z). Same as Laplace for continuous systems.

questions.prompt: The region of convergence (ROC) for the Z-transform of a causal (right-sided) signal is:
choices: C. On the unit circle only
questions.explanation: Causal (right-sided) sequence: ROC is |z| > r (exterior of a circle). Anti-causal: ROC is |z| < r.

questions.prompt: The Z-transform of n·u[n] (discrete ramp) is:
questions.explanation: Z{n·u[n]} = z/(z−1)². Analogous to L{t} = 1/s².

questions.prompt: Partial fraction expansion of X(z)/z (rather than X(z)) is used to:
choices: A. Make it easier to find inverse Z-transforms using standard tables
D. Convert from continuous to discrete time
questions.explanation: Expand X(z)/z, then multiply result by z. This avoids having residues involving z in the numerator and simplifies table lookup.

questions.prompt: The bilinear transform (Tustin's method) maps the s-plane to the z-plane via:
questions.explanation: Bilinear (Tustin): s = (2/T)(z−1)/(z+1). Maps the jω axis to the unit circle exactly; used to design digital filters from analog prototypes.

questions.prompt: An FIR (finite impulse response) filter differs from an IIR filter in that it:
choices: B. Has no feedback; all poles at z = 0; always stable
D. Cannot be designed from an analog prototype
questions.explanation: FIR: no recursive (feedback) terms → all poles at origin → always stable. IIR: has poles (recursive), potentially unstable.

questions.prompt: The initial value theorem for Z-transforms gives x[0] = ?
questions.explanation: IVT for Z: x[0] = lim_{z→∞} X(z).

questions.prompt: The sampling theorem (Nyquist) states that to reconstruct a signal of bandwidth B, the sampling rate must be:
questions.explanation: Nyquist–Shannon: f_s > 2B (Nyquist rate). Sampling below this causes aliasing.

questions.prompt: Which are valid methods to compute the inverse Z-transform? (Select ALL that apply.)
questions.explanation: All four methods are valid for inverse Z-transform. Partial fractions and tables are most common in engineering practice.

questions.prompt: Aliasing in discrete-time systems occurs when:
choices: A. The signal bandwidth exceeds f_s/2
D. The filter is linear phase
questions.explanation: Aliasing: frequency content above f_s/2 (Nyquist frequency) folds back into the baseband, distorting the sampled signal.

questions.prompt: The Z-transform of cos(ω₀n)u[n] is:
questions.explanation: Z{cos(ω₀n)u[n]} = z(z−cos ω₀)/(z²−2z cos ω₀+1). Z{sin(ω₀n)u[n]} = z sin ω₀/(z²−2z cos ω₀+1).

questions.prompt: A causal sequence has Z-transform X(z) = 5z/(z−0.5). Using the final value theorem, x[∞] = ?
questions.explanation: FVT: x[∞] = lim_{z→1}(z−1)X(z) = lim_{z→1}(z−1)·5z/(z−0.5) = (1−1)·5/0.5 = 0. The signal decays to zero (|pole| = 0.5 < 1).

questions.prompt: The DFT (Discrete Fourier Transform) evaluates the Z-transform at:
choices: A. N equally spaced points on the unit circle
B. All points inside the unit circle
questions.explanation: DFT: X[k] = X(z)|_{z=e^(j2πk/N)}, k=0,…,N−1. N equally-spaced points on the unit circle.

questions.prompt: The FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) reduces DFT computation complexity from O(N²) to:
questions.explanation: FFT (Cooley-Tukey): O(N log₂ N) for N = power of 2. Dramatic speedup over direct DFT for large N.

questions.prompt: A digital filter has poles at z = 0.9e^(±jπ/4). This filter is:
choices: B. Stable — poles are inside the unit circle
questions.explanation: |z| = 0.9 < 1 → poles inside unit circle → stable. The complex conjugate pair gives a bandpass characteristic near ω = π/4.

questions.prompt: If X(z) = z/(z-0.8) for a causal sequence, the corresponding time-domain sequence is:
questions.explanation: Standard pair: Z{a^n u[n]} = z/(z-a). Here a=0.8, so x[n]=0.8^n u[n].
